coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots functionalized g-C3N4 nanosheets as enhanced visible-light photocatalysts for water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Gen
Huang, Jiaming
Wang, Niuniu
Huang, Jiale
Zheng, Yanmei
Zhan, Guowu
Li, Qingbiao
coredata.dc:description: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a representative photocatalyst, but the photocatalytic activity is often limited because of the easy recombination and slow migration of photogenerated charges. Herein, we have successfully prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs) functionalized g-C3N4 (CCN) by a simple thermal polymerization method. Besides, the CQDs serving as a photosensitizer can enlarge the absorption range of visible light of the composite material. As a result, under the irradiation of visible light, the optimized CCN showed superior catalytic activity with a high H2 production rate of 2321 μmol·g−1·h−1, which was almost about 7.4 times higher than that of bulk g-C3N4. Based on the systematic characterization and density functional theory (DFT) calculation, it was proved that the efficiencies of the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges were enhanced by the micro-regional heterostructure of CQDs and g-C3N4.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs
g-C3N4
Photocatalysis
H2 evolution
DFT calculation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DFT calculation and possible photocatalytic mechanism

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation procedure of CCN-x (x = 10, 30, or 50). (b) SEM and (c,d) TEM images of CCN-10. (e) High resolution TEM image of one CQD present in CCN-10.
XRD patterns of pure g-C3N4 and CCN-x (x = 10, 30, 50).
FT-IR spectra of g-C3N4 and CCN-10.
(a) XPS survey spectra of g-C3N4 and CCN-10. (b) High-resolution C1s spectra of g-C3N4 and CCN-10. (c) High-resolution N1s spectra of g-C3N4 and CCN-10.
Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and the corresponding pore-size distribution curves (inset) of g-C3N4 and CCN-10.
(a) UV–vis DRS and Tauc plots (inset) of g-C3N4 and CCN-x (x = 10, 30, 50). (b–c) XPS valence band spectra and (d–e) Mott-Schottky plots of g-C3N4 and CCN-10. (f) Band structure of g-C3N4 and CCN-10.

(a) PL spectra with an excitation (λex) wavelength of 300 nm, (b) time-resolved transient PL decay, (c) transient photocurrent response of g-C3N4 and CCN-x (x = 10, 30, 50) and (d) electrochemical impedance spectra of g-C3N4 and CCN-10.
(a) Photocatalytic H2 evolution rate over different samples and (b) photocatalytic H2 evolution stability test of CCN-10 under visible light (λ ≥ 420 nm).
Calculated band structure and corresponding DOS of (a, c) g-C3N4 and (b, d) CCN.

Calculated spatial distributions of HOMO (green) and LUMO (blue) for (a,c) g-C3N4 and (b,d) CCN (wherein, the isosurface is 0.05 e Å−3). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
A plausible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution in CCN.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots functionalized g-C3N4 nanosheets as enhanced visible-light photocatalysts for water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, Fujian, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, Fujian, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Huaqiao University, Xiamen 361021, Fujian, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Huaqiao University, Xiamen 361021, Fujian, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, Fujian, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, Fujian, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is a representative photocatalyst, but the photocatalytic activity is often limited because of the easy recombination and slow migration of photogenerated charges. Herein, we have successfully prepared carbon quantum dots (CQDs) functionalized g-C3N4 (CCN) by a simple thermal polymerization method. Besides, the CQDs serving as a photosensitizer can enlarge the absorption range of visible light of the composite material. As a result, under the irradiation of visible light, the optimized CCN showed superior catalytic activity with a high H2 production rate of 2321 μmol·g−1·h−1, which was almost about 7.4 times higher than that of bulk g-C3N4. Based on the systematic characterization and density functional theory (DFT) calculation, it was proved that the efficiencies of the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges were enhanced by the micro-regional heterostructure of CQDs and g-C3N4.

ce:section.ce:para: The over-consumption of fossil energy and serious environmental pollution are two critical issues for the sustainable social development [1–3]. Developing alternative clean energy is an ideal way to deal with these problems. At present, there are many environment-friendly energy sources, such as wind, geothermal energy, biomass, and solar energy, etc. [4]. Among them, splitting water by using solar energy to obtain renewable hydrogen is one of the most effective methods. Moreover, hydrogen has an ideal heat value (120–142 MJ·kg−1), which is about 2.5, 3.0, and 4.5 times than that of natural gas, gasoline, and coal, respectively [5]. Accordingly, a series of semiconductor photocatalysts have been explored, such as metal oxides [6,7], metal sulfides [8,9], organic semiconductors [10], and so on. However, the low efficiency of photogenerated charge separation and the low absorption of visible light greatly limit the catalytic activity of such semiconductor photocatalysts.

To date, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), as an organic semiconductor, has attracted worldwide research attention due to its high thermal and chemical stabilities, suitable bandgap, and low production cost with earth-abundant C and N elements [11]. Especially, π-conjugated graphitic planes can be formed by the tri-s-triazine ring units, which is assembled by sp2 hybridization [12]. It is worth noting that the bridged nitrogen atoms with three coordination neither participate in the valence band nor the conduction band, making it difficult for the photogenerated charge to transfer from one tri-s-triazine ring unit to another [13]. Therefore, photogenerated electrons and holes are easily bound in each tri-s-triazine ring, which fail to separate effectively, resulting in poor photocatalytic activity. In addition, the adjacent conjugated planes in the bulk g-C3N4 are stacked by a weak van der Waals force, which limits the electronic coupling between the two planes [14]. In order to circumvent these limitations and increase the photocatalytic activity, a variety of strategies have been developed, for instance noble metal deposition [15–17], doping different elements [18,19], dye sensitization [20–22], controlled morphology [23–25], and coupling with another semiconductor [26–28], etc.

Recently, a number of researchers have pay intensive attention to carbon quantum dots (CQDs) because of the unique physicochemical and photoelectric properties [29]. In particular, efficient light absorption, adjustable photoluminescence (PL), up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL), and excellent photogenerated electron transfer altogether contribute CQDs with great application potential in the field of photocatalysis [30–32]. Thus, some pioneering studies have been carried out that decorating the CQDs on the surface of BiOCOOH and g-C3N4 to enhance the photocatalytic degradation of diclofenac, with the enhanced degradation rate of about 4.64 and 15 times than that of pure BiOCOOH and g-C3N4 [33,34]. For instance, Mou et al. have coupled the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and g-C3N4 by π-p-π connection, which efficiently promotes charge separation and the visible light absorption [35]. Besides, citric acid and glucose can also be used as a carbon source to directly thermally polymerize with urea to obtain CQDs-doped g-C3N4 composite photocatalysts [36,37]. Wang et al. have implanted the CQDs on the surface plane of g-C3N4 nanotubes with the method of thermal polymerization of freeze-dried precursor [38]. However, the improvement of the activity of CQDs towards hydrogen production via water splitting is limited. Moreover, theoretical research on CQDs doped g-C3N4 is insufficient; particularly, there is no in-depth explanation of the influence of CQDs on the electronic structure of g-C3N4.

Herein, we first prepared CQDs functionalized with amino groups on the surface by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea. In the thermal polymerization, through the deamination reaction, the CQDs and g-C3N4 were highly integrated, possibly leading to a CQDs-implanted g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst. As the composite was used for water splitting, the micro-regional heterostructure of CQDs and g-C3N4 could promote the separation and transfer of photogenerated charges, while CQDs not only acted as photosensitizers to harvest more visible light but also increased the specific surface area of the photocatalyst, providing more active sites. The DFT calculation was also applied to prove the origin of the enhanced performance due to CQDs. Therefore, the implanting of CQDs markedly enhances the activity of g-C3N4 to produce hydrogen by splitting water under visible light.

Urea (AR), citric acid (AR), and H2PtCl6·6H2O (AR) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Triethanolamine (AR) was obtained from Xilong Scientific Co.Ltd. (Guangdong, China). All the reagents were used as purchased without further purification treatment.

The CQDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method based on previous studies with some modifications [34,39]. In detail, citric acid (15 g) and urea (5 g) were dissolved in deionized water (75 mL). Then, the solution was transferred to a 150 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. Moreover, the suspension was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 10 min to remove the bulk particles. Finally, the CQDs solution (concentration of 127.3 mg/mL) was stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C.

For the preparation of CQDs functionalized g-C3N4 (viz., CCN), 10 g of urea was mixed with different amounts of CQDs (0 μL, 10 μL, 30 μL, and 50 μL) in 30 mL of water, which was then magnetically stirred for 6 h, then stood for 12 h to form a light transparent blue solution, and finally dried at 60 °C. The precursors were then calcined at 550 °C for 3 h and the heating rate was 15 °C/min. The obtained photocatalysts were denoted as CCN-x, x represents the amounts of CQDs. If no CQDs were added, then g-C3N4 was obtained.
The detailed information about characterization methods, photocatalytic water splitting test, photoelectrochemical measurements, and DFT calculation are shown in the Supporting information.

CCN-x were synthesized as depicted schematically in Fig. 1a. The CCN-x composites were obtained by hydrothermal treatment using urea and different amounts of CQDs. SEM and TEM were used to obtain the morphology and microstructure of the samples. Fig. 1b shows that the SEM image of CCN-10, which is composed of numerous ultrathin paper-fold sheets with several micrometers. This is the typical structure characteristic of g-C3N4, similar to other reports [34,40,41]. As shown in Fig. 1c–e, CQDs with a diameter of 8–10 nm are evenly distributed in the g-C3N4. The measured lattice fringe of 0.22 nm can be ascribed to the (101) plane of graphite carbon [38].

The XRD patterns of the catalyst samples were measured and shown in Fig. 2 . It could be observed that all the samples exhibited two similar distinct diffraction peaks at around 12.9° and 27.6°, which are indexed to (100) and (002) planes of g-C3N4. Among them, the stronger peak at 27.6° is related to the interlayer-stacking structure of the conjugated aromatic system with the distance around 0.323 nm, while the weaker one at 12.9° originates from the in-plane repeating tri-s-triazine ring structure units with the distance around 0.686 nm [42,43]. As increasing the loading amount of CQDs, the intensities of both diffraction peaks are gradually reduced, which is due to the fact that the existence of CQDs impedes the thermopolymerization of tri-s-triazine units and the stacking of g-C3N4 sheets [38].

Furthermore, the FT-IR spectra of the catalysts are shown in Fig. 3 . The results show that the pure g-C3N4 and CCN-10 have similar FT-IR spectra. The wide band at 3000–3400 cm−1 represents the NH group, while the OH band is due to the uncondensed amino groups and adsorbed hydroxyl groups on the surface [44]. The peaks at 1200–1600 cm−1 and 807 cm−1 belong to the stretching and breathing modes of the triazine units [45] [46]. Thus, it can be concluded that adding a small amount of CQDs did not significantly change the original framework of g-C3N4.

In order to obtain the proportion of different elements and chemical states in bulk g-C3N4 and CCN-10, the XPS spectra of both samples were recorded. The survey spectra in Fig. 4a demonstrates that both bulk g-C3N4 and CCN-10 are composed of C, N, and O. It should be noted that a small amount of oxygen is often detected in g-C3N4 prepared with urea [47]. The elemental analyzer test revealed that the C and N atom ratio of CCN-10 is higher than that of g-C3N4 (0.57 vs 0.55), which is probably due to the addition of carbon quantum dots. As shown in Fig. 4b, g-C3N4 and CCN-10 have a similar C 1s spectra. There are two obvious peaks in the spectra, located at binding energy (B.E.) of 284.8 and 288.2 eV, which belong to CC bonds of graphitic carbon and sp2-hybridized in the aromatic structure (N-C=N), respectively [38]. Interestingly, a new peak located at B.E. of 286.4 eV is observed for CCN-10 corresponding to the CO species due to CQDs. It should be noted that the relative amount of the CC (21.71%) of CCN-10 is much bigger than that (17.05%) of g-C3N4, confirming the successful incorporation of CQDs (Table S1). The N 1s spectra (Fig. 4c) of g-C3N4 and CCN-10 exhibit four peaks at 398.2 eV, 399.6 eV, 400.8 eV, and 404.2 eV, which are associated with the sp2-hybridized N (C-N=C), N-(C)3 groups, amino groups(NH), and the π excitations, respectively [48]. The intensities of distinct N species did not change significantly, which indicates that the skeleton structure of g-C3N4 is retained (Table S1).

To further characterize the specific surface area and pore structures of the photocatalysts, the BET surface areas were investigated by N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. As can be seen in Fig. 5 and Table S2, all the catalysts exhibited type IV curves with type H3 hysteresis loops. Additionally, it should be noted that the BET surface area of CCN-10 is about 1.56 times than bulk g-C3N4 (116.88 m2/g vs 74.93 m2/g), resultant in the improvement of photocatalytic performance by providing more active sites (vide infra).

In order to better analyze the optical properties of bulk g-C3N4 and CCN-x, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were detected (Fig. 6a). Results show that the light absorption of g-C3N4 is significantly enhanced after the introduction of CQDs, especially in the visible light range. Particularly, the absorption edge of g-C3N4 is about 452 nm, corresponding to the bandgap of 2.74 eV. Interestingly, the red-shift of the absorption edge of CCN-10 means a narrower bandgap of 2.62 eV than that of g-C3N4. Furthermore, the XPS valence band spectra were carried out (Fig. 6b–c). Apparently, the maxima of valance bands (VB) of g-C3N4 and CCN-10 are calculated as 1.74 eV and 1.66 eV referred to the Fermi energy, respectively. The shift of VB maximum is ascribed to the change of the electronic structure. From Mott-Schottky analysis (Fig. 6d–e), we can obtain flat band potential (Efb) of g-C3N4 and CCN-10 is about −0.9 and −0.86 eV with reference to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE). Generally, the flat band potential can be used to calculate the conduction band (CB) potential (Ec) by using the equation Ec = Efb − 0.1 eV. The conduction band shift of 0.04 eV for g-C3N4 and CCN-10 (−1.00 eV for g-C3N4 and −0.96 eV for CCN-10) can be observed. Therefore, a schematic diagram of the energy band structure of g-C3N4 and CCN-10 is shown in Fig. 6f. It is concluded that CCN-10 has a smaller bandgap than that of g-C3N4, which is more beneficial to the photocatalytic water splitting to the H2 evolution.

To further understand the charge carriers separation and recombination behavior, the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra were performed. As shown in Fig. 7a, the emission peak of CCN-x (x = 10, 30, 50) are remarkably suppressed as compared to g-C3N4, indicating that the spatial separation of electrons and holes produced by optical excitation is improved by implanting CQDs. The lifetimes of the charge carriers were measured by time-resolved fluorescence (Fig. 7b and Table S3). It is worth noting that the average PL lifetime of CCN-x is shorter than that of g-C3N4, suggesting that photo-excited electrons are more quickly transferred to the surface of the catalyst and then captured by reactive substrates, which can then drive photocatalytic hydrogen evolution [49,50]. Fig. 7c illustrates that the photocurrent density of CCN-10 which is obviously higher compared to that of bulk g-C3N4, further confirming the more efficient separation of photo-excited electrons and holes in CCN-10. Besides, the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) in Fig. 7d displays that CCN-10 exhibits a much smaller radius of the semicircular Nyquist plots than that of bulk g-C3N4, demonstrating that the interface charge transport of CCN-10 can be effectively promoted compared to bulk g-C3N4.

The photocatalytic H2 generation activity by visible-light-induced water splitting of all the samples was evaluated with 10 vol% TEOA as sacrificial reagents under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm). Prior to the tests, 1 wt% Pt was loaded on the surface of the catalyst as a co-catalyst by the in-situ photo-deposition approach. As shown in Fig. 8a, all the CCN-x samples remarkably enhance the H2 generation performance. The average H2 evolution rate of CCN-10 reaches 2321 μmol·g−1·h−1, which is approximate 7.4 times higher than that of bulk g-C3N4 (313 μmol·g−1·h−1). The photocatalytic stability test of CCN-10 was assessed by continuous visible light irradiation over five cycles for 25 h (Fig. 8b). Remarkably, there is no obvious reduction of the H2 production rate after five cycles, suggesting the high stability of CCN-10 against the photo-corrosion. The XRD and FT-IR spectra for CCN-10 before and after 25 h photocatalytic experiments were compared in Figs. S5, 6, indicating that almost no change in the physicochemical properties of CCN-10. Therefore, all the results demonstrate that CCN-10 possesses excellent photocatalytic activity and chemical stability during the H2 evolution reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DFT calculation and possible photocatalytic mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the effect of the CQDs on the electronic structure of g-C3N4, the band structure, the density of states (DOS), and HOMO-LUMO transition of the bulk g-C3N4 and CCN model catalysts were calculated via the first-principles pseudopotential methods based on DFT. For the structure of g-C3N4, there are two C sites and three N sites, which are named as C1, C2, N1, N2, and N3, respectively (Fig. S7). The calculated results of band structures of g-C3N4 and CCN are shown in Fig. 9a, b. The DOS of g-C3N4 and CCN are presented in Fig. 9c and d, respectively. It shows that the bandgap of bulk g-C3N4 decreases from 2.76 to 2.58 eV after introducing the CQDs, which matches well with the experimental results in Fig. 6f. Also, the donor level composed of C_p orbitals appears at −1.5 eV above the valance band, consistent with the increasing absorption tail for CCN in Fig. 6a. The DOS diagram shows that the valence band of g-C3N4 and CCN is mainly composed of N_p orbitals, while the conduction band is composed of C_p and N_p. This is mainly because that the electronegativity of nitrogen is greater than that of carbon.

The HOMO and LUMO of g-C3N4 are shown in Fig. 10 a, c, it can be found that HOMO occupies all N2 atoms, while LUMO is distributed on C1, C2, N1, and N2 atoms, which is consistent with the result of the DOS. As shown in Fig. S8, the distance from the N_p orbit of the bridged nitrogen (N3_g-C3N4) atom with three coordination to the Fermi level is 4.2 eV, making it difficult for the photogenerated charge transferring from one tri-s-triazine ring unit to another through the bridged nitrogen atom [13]. Therefore, photogenerated electrons and holes are bound in each tri-s-triazine ring unit, making them easy to recombine, resulting in poor photocatalytic activity. Interestingly, the N_p orbital of the bridged nitrogen atom near the CQDs (N3_CCN) is closer to the Fermi level than that of g-C3N4 and contributes partially to the HOMO. As can be seen from Fig. S9, the donor energy level is mainly contributed by CQDs, agreed well with the DOS results.

Based on the above results, a plausible reaction mechanism of CCN for photocatalytic H2 evolution is proposed in Fig. 11 . Under the irradiation of visible light, electrons at the donor level of CQDs are easily excited to the conduction band, so the bandgap of the composite catalyst is reduced. As more visible light can be harvested, more photo-generated charges are generated to participate in hydrogen production reaction. On the other hand, the micro-regional heterostructures of CQDs and g-C3N4 can change the charges distribution of the surrounding atoms of g-C3N4, promote the transfer of photogenerated charges between tri-s-triazine ring units, and thus reduce effectively the recombination of photogenerated charges. Therefore, more photogenerated electrons migrate to the surface of the catalyst and the supported platinum to participate in the hydrogen production reaction, meanwhile, TEOA, as a sacrificial reagent, can consume holes in the valence band and enhance the separation of photogenerated carriers.

In summary, CQDs functionalized g-C3N4 (CCN) has been successfully synthesized by a facile one-step thermal copolymerization of CQDs and urea precursors. Under visible light irradiation, the average H2 evolution rate of CCN-10 reaches 2321 μmol·g−1·h−1, which is approximate 7.4 times higher than that of bulk g-C3N4. The micro-heterojunction of CCN-x not only promotes the transfer and reduces the recombination of photogenerated charges, but also optimizes the energy band structure and increases visible light harvesting. Besides, the DFT theoretical calculation shows that the donor level provided by the CQDs can inject more photogenerated charges into the g-C3N4 and reduce the bandgap, which is consistent with the experimental results. Furthermore, the increased specific surface area of CCN can provide more active sites for H2 evolution. To sum up, this work may provide a new route to design and synthesize highly efficient and low-cost metal-free photocatalysts, which can be extended to many other fields such as water purification, environmental remediation, energy conversion, and electro-optical devices.
Gen Li: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - Original Draft.
Jiaming Huang: Visualization, Investigation, Writing - Original Draft.
Niuniu Wang: Validation, Investigation, Writing - Original Draft.
Jiale Huang: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - Review & Editing.
Yanmei Zheng: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing - Review & Editing.
Guowu Zhan: Methodology, Investigation, Writing - Review & Editing.
Qingbiao Li: Investigation, Writing - Review & Editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2020J01023), the Science and Technology Project of Xiamen City (3502Z20183009), the National Key Research and Development Program (2019YFC1805801).
